glossary.definition: A field that deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020).

glossary.definition: mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows.
glossary.sentence: Much less we know about animal intelligence . It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows.

glossary.definition: not based on a brain and nervous system; they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a “sense of place” and an awareness of the neighbourhood.
glossary.sentence: It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system (Parise, Gagliano and Souza, 2020) . At the same time, plants possess many forms of intellectual performance, such as (Gagliano, 2015) : they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a “sense of place” and an awareness of the neighbourhood.

glossary.definition: an approach that underlies systems that think humanly and considers human being to be an information processing system.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system.

glossary.definition: systems that are based on logics.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics .

glossary.definition: an approach that underlies systems that act humanly.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence, which underlies systems that act humanly.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.
glossary.sentence: In this context, an agent is any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.

glossary.definition: one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: A chapter of artificial intelligence, contributed to by linguistics, related to how language is related to thinking and used in data processing machines.
glossary.sentence: Studies of how language is related to thinking contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation in the field of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system
glossary.sentence: It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: a capability to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions, required to pass the Turing test.
glossary.sentence: automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions

glossary.definition: A method of data analysis that automates analytical model building, and a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: The description provided by SAS (n. d.) explains the concept in straightforward words: "Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention."

glossary.definition: A test that brings forward requirements for a computer to have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: The scientific field whose philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge.

glossary.definition: The scientific field that provided tools for mathematical formalization in computing, logics, and probability theory, including tools to work with logical statements and probabilistic judgments, and foundations for reasoning algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory.

glossary.definition: The scientific field that offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: The scientific field related to research on how the human brain processes information, leading to the emergence of artificial neural networks that simulate human brain neurons for tasks like classification and pattern recognition.
glossary.sentence: Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information.

glossary.definition: biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.”

glossary.definition: The scientific field whose psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.
glossary.sentence: Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.

glossary.definition: The scientific field that provided artefacts, such as progress in computer speed and memory capacity, which made possible the development of artificial intelligence applications.
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.

glossary.term: Control science and cybernetics (contribution to AI)
glossary.definition: The scientific field whose main contribution to artificial intelligence is the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer.
glossary.sentence: Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer.

glossary.definition: The scientific field that showed the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines, and whose studies contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation in artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Linguistics - Linguists showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines.

glossary.definition: A chapter of artificial intelligence that enables a computer to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A chapter of artificial intelligence that enables a computer to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: Related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions (Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems.

glossary.definition: Related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)

glossary.definition: A hypothesis that defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved, formulated as: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action”.
glossary.sentence: It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976).

glossary.definition: A physical system capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Meaningful physical patterns that could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.

glossary.term: symbolic structures (in a physical symbol system)
glossary.definition: Structures which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way and depict important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way.

glossary.definition: Actions such as constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying, which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: A famous example of criticism against the physical symbol system hypothesis, where a physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols without having intelligence, as illustrated by a person in a room following instructions to process Chinese symbols without understanding them.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism.

glossary.definition: connected processing units that mimic the neurons of the human brain
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: an approach to artificial intelligence that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: an approach that treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated, related to the development of computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities and is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: an approach that focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales, asserting that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales” ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) .

glossary.definition: a paradigm under new artificial intelligence that considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: a way of structural organization of data about the real world
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world;

glossary.definition: places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others
glossary.sentence: data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others;

glossary.definition: ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal
glossary.sentence: algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: a formal or mathematical representation
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.term: initial state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: a state we are in at present
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.term: goal state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: a state we would like to reach
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: A graph representing the states of a game, which can be complete or partial.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: corresponding to discrete states of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes (the description given below is partly based on the information given in (Luger, 2009)) : nodes corresponding to discrete states of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: The topmost node in a graph to which heuristic measures are propagated level by level.
glossary.sentence: When heuristic measures have been assigned to the leaf nodes, we propagate them, level by level, up to the root node (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process and do not have descendants
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process and have both ancestors and descendants
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: represent transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: arcs that represent transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: represented by arcs in a state space graph, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph actions are represented by arcs, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving.

glossary.definition: a part of our real world, which includes states and actions.
glossary.sentence: So, a problem-solving process, which is a part of our real world, includes states and actions.

glossary.definition: represented by nodes in a state space graph, and included in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph represents states of the problem-solving process by nodes and actions by arcs.

glossary.definition: represent states of the problem-solving process in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph represents states of the problem-solving process by nodes and actions by arcs.

glossary.definition: represent actions in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph represents states of the problem-solving process by nodes and actions by arcs.

glossary.definition: A system equipped with an algorithm that will process the graph, with the aim of imitating game playing and striving to win the game.
glossary.sentence: We also need to equip an intelligent computer system with an algorithm that will process the graph, with the aim of imitating game playing and striving to win the game (Figure 1).

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-person games with perfect information
glossary.definition: a simple type of game where two players compete, make alternate moves, have complete information, no randomness, and one player's loss equals another's gain for a total sum of zero.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information.

glossary.definition: a category of games based on the number of players participating.
glossary.sentence: based on the number of players participating in a game - two-player games vs. multi-player games;

glossary.definition: a category of games based on the number of players participating.
glossary.sentence: based on the number of players participating in a game - two-player games vs. multi-player games;

glossary.definition: refers to the availability of complete information about the course of the game for both players.
glossary.sentence: based on the availability of information about the course of the game for both players - perfect information vs. imperfect information;

glossary.definition: refers to the availability of only part of the information about the course of the game for the players.
glossary.sentence: based on the availability of information about the course of the game for both players - perfect information vs. imperfect information;

glossary.definition: a category of games based on the level of randomness associated with a game, where random factors are not involved.
glossary.sentence: based on the level of randomness associated with a game - deterministic vs. stochastic games, and others.

glossary.definition: a category of games based on the level of randomness associated with a game, where a die or other random factors could be used.
glossary.sentence: based on the level of randomness associated with a game - deterministic vs. stochastic games, and others.

glossary.term: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: games like checkers, tic-tac-toe, and chess, where both players have all information about the game's course and no random factors are involved.
glossary.sentence: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information, such as checkers, tic-tac-toe and chess, in which both players have all the information about the course of the game (for example, which objects or figures are still available, which moves have been performed and which next moves are possible), and random factors are not involved.

glossary.term: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: games like backgammon, where both players are aware of what is happening on the playing board, but a die is used, preventing precise prediction of the next move's score.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.

glossary.term: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information
glossary.definition: games like bridge and poker, where part of the game information is hidden from players and a die may be used to introduce randomness.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: the state that we are in at present in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: First, we determine the state that we are in at present (the initial state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: the state we would like to reach in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: Then, we decide on the state we would like to reach (the goal state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: a series of states passed through when performing actions to eliminate the discrepancy between initial and goal states in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: To eliminate the discrepancy between these states, we perform actions and pass through a series of intermediate states (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: discrete states of the game, changing by making legal moves.
glossary.sentence: Thus, playing a game involves changing between game states by making legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: actions that change between game states.
glossary.sentence: Thus, playing a game involves changing between game states by making legal moves in the game.

glossary.definition: clearly distinguishable distinctions in a state space graph where one or more nodes are located, displaying states from which only one of the two players can make a move.
glossary.sentence: Node levels are clearly distinguishable in the state space graph. One or more nodes are located at each level, and these display states of the game from which only one of the two players can make a move.

glossary.term: parameters that characterise the states in the game
glossary.definition: properties of the game and its course, related to computer implementation, whose values change after making a move, allowing players to track the game's course by determining a specific state at a specific moment in time.
glossary.sentence: These parameters can be described as follows: they are related to a computer implementation of a game; they correspond to the properties of the game and its course, in which the values change after making a move during the course of the game; the values of the parameters allow the players to track the course of the game, since they determine a specific state in the game at a specific moment in time;

glossary.definition: nodes at the same level that have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game.
glossary.sentence: Nodes at the same level are identical if they have the same values for all of the parameters that characterise the states of the game.

glossary.definition: A process that sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph while searching for a solution path, taking input data such as a search start node and a search goal node, and providing a solution path as output.
glossary.sentence: The search principles explained previously have shown that while searching for a solution path, the search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The initial node from which the search begins.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A.

glossary.definition: The node that the algorithm checks if it is, and if found among the nodes in the OPEN list, the algorithm stops its operation.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node.

glossary.definition: The output provided by search algorithms, which can be reconstructed when the search goal node is found.
glossary.sentence: The algorithms provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: Nodes that the search algorithm has already generated, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: A type of search where the process begins with a problem description or given data (a pattern) added to the working memory.
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: A technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred, typically used for problems of interpretation.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of search where a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory, and the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches this pattern against the THEN part of production rules.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A process where an expert system has a goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it, corresponding to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, where if the search goal node is not reached by the current path, it is necessary to backtrack to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants/ancestors and to try another path.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that receive the search start node and search goal node as input, use a state space graph to find a problem solution, provide a solution path as output, and involve three main operational steps: selecting, checking, and expanding the current node.
glossary.sentence: They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: Another name for uninformed search, which describes algorithms that explore the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Another name for uninformed search, which describes algorithms that explore the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that receive an additional input, the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes (a heuristic evaluation function), and have additional steps after expanding the current node to evaluate generated nodes and select or order them according to heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that have three main operational steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: They have three main operational steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order.
glossary.sentence: Search algorithms are often implemented using a data structure called a list. A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list where the search start node is added, and later, selected best nodes are added.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A list that is empty initially, and where expanded or generated but not best nodes are moved.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: A search method used by the algorithm that starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011), in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure that works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: The principle by which a stack works, where the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A problem where, in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.
glossary.sentence: Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths . It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that overcomes the problem of going along indefinitely deep paths by checking if a node's depth is lower than the depth limit, but lacks guidelines on choosing this limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: A limit on the depth of nodes to be explored, used by Depth-Limited Search to overcome indefinitely deep paths.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if its depth is lower than the depth limit.

glossary.definition: Labels that allow the algorithm to understand if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.
glossary.sentence: Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: The node located on the first position in the OPEN list that the algorithm takes.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: A node that has had its descendants generated and is moved to the CLOSED list.
glossary.sentence: The node D is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node.

glossary.definition: The method by which Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search explore the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Both algorithms considered so far—Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search—explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches, which, when searching in a level-by-level fashion, finds the shortest path sooner because it consists of a smaller number of arcs, meaning the search goal node is located at higher levels, but it has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.

glossary.definition: Nodes generated by expanding a current node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node D by generating its descendant - the node J.

glossary.definition: Nodes from which a current node was generated, depending on the search direction.
glossary.sentence: It generates its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: A type of data structure used in Breadth-First Search where new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list, working according to the 'First in, First Out' principle.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A principle according to which a queue works, meaning the first data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A queue works according to the 'First in, First Out' principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed.

glossary.definition: A node reached by the algorithm where a precise heuristic measure can be assigned, such as a state where the maximiser wins the game.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm reaches a leaf node. It assigns a precise heuristic measure of 1 to the leaf node, since this is a state in which the maximiser wins the game with a higher score.

glossary.definition: A modification of Breadth-First Search that reduces the consumption of computational resources (search time and computer memory) by performing a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.

glossary.definition: Experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way, serving as a rule of thumb that will not give the perfect outcome but will nevertheless usually lead to a good or at least acceptable outcome with much less effort. In state space search, it provides rules for choosing those paths in a state space graph that are most likely to lead to an acceptable problem solution.
glossary.sentence: Heuristics (or heuristic knowledge) are experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: Problem-specific knowledge, often based on experience, used to solve problems.
glossary.sentence: To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience.

glossary.definition: A function used to find the most promising moves when processing a partial state space graph.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, and use a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves.

glossary.definition: Values obtained for all leaf nodes and then propagated level by level up to the root node.
glossary.sentence: The next step is to obtain heuristic measures for all leaf nodes.

glossary.definition: An element in a state space graph to which heuristic measures are assigned, and which are connected by arcs.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph, we have only nodes and the arcs connecting them.

glossary.definition: A representation of the quality of a node n, calculated as the sum of g(n) and h(n), where g(n) is the distance between the search start node and node n, and h(n) is a heuristic measure estimating the distance between node n and the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n, a value that allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n, or the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node, allowing the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: An element characterizing states in the problem-solving process, which can be included in a heuristic evaluation function, often with an associated weight and heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs per node in a state space graph, acquired by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes.
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.term: Depth of the state space graph (L)
glossary.definition: Usually the length of the longest path in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: A state space graph where it is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources, and thus it is generated to a specified depth during the operation of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: Only a part of a state space graph (a partly available state space graph) . It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources.

glossary.definition: A situation where, when a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, which may lead to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: A state space graph for which computational resources are sufficient to store and process it.
glossary.sentence: A full state space graph (a fully available state space graph) . Computational resources are enough to store and process such a graph.

glossary.definition: A feature of states that, when applied, allows for considering fewer distinct states (e.g., corner, centre, side in Tic-tac-toe) because their specific position does not matter, leading to a reduction in the number of states in a game tree.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12).

glossary.definition: A search algorithm whose idea is to always move to the best node in the state space graph, climbing up to the best heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A value given to nodes by the heuristic evaluation function, where a smaller measure can represent a better quality of node.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic measures are given close to nodes, and a smaller heuristic measure represents a better quality of node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always selects the best node, determined by heuristic measures, and is able to find the search goal node very quickly if the heuristic evaluation function is well-defined.
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that Hill Climbing always selects the best node.

glossary.definition: A node determined by the heuristic measures of nodes, which Hill Climbing always selects.
glossary.sentence: The best node is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far in each iteration, keeps all previously found nodes, and allows switching between different paths in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: A data structure that implements the OPEN list and works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that is a compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search, selecting a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: A defined parameter according to which the algorithm selects the best nodes based on heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: According to the defined beam width, the algorithm selects the two best nodes based on the heuristic measures.

glossary.term: deterministic two-player zero-sum game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game whose algorithm selection for implementation depends on the availability of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The selection of an algorithm for the implementation of a deterministic two-player zero-sum game of perfect information depends on the availability of the state space graph, i.e. whether it is completely or partially available.

glossary.definition: A graph that includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A more complex case of a state space graph where the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: The entity whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have the measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: The entity whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the minimiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be smaller, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the current node is located, used in assigning intermediate heuristic measures or in determining the maximum heuristic measure from direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the current node is located, used in assigning precise heuristic measures based on descendants' measures.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: The outcome of a game, such as 1 for a maximiser win, −1 for a minimiser win, or 0 for a draw.
glossary.sentence: In this case, we have a game result of 1, meaning that if both players play rationally then the maximiser will win the game.

glossary.definition: A path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the conditions that all nodes in the path are closely related and have the same heuristic measures equal to the game result.
glossary.sentence: A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that provides game results, which can be used to identify winning paths.
glossary.sentence: This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: Nodes in a graph for which heuristic measures are initially obtained.
glossary.sentence: The next step is to obtain heuristic measures for all leaf nodes.

glossary.definition: A node connected below another node in a graph.
glossary.sentence: The first node from the left has only one descendant, and its value is therefore assigned to this node.

glossary.definition: Paths that show the most likely course of the game found based on the game result.
glossary.sentence: The winning paths that show the most likely course of the game found based on the game result (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: Resources required for evaluating all nodes in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The minimax algorithm pursues all of the branches and evaluates all of the nodes in the state space graph, and may therefore require significant computational resources.

glossary.definition: A characteristic of a state space graph, where a low value makes the minimax algorithm good.
glossary.sentence: According to Jones (2009), it is a good algorithm for a state space graph with a low depth and branching factor, but can consume large amounts of storage for more complex problems.

glossary.definition: A characteristic of a state space graph, where a low value makes the minimax algorithm good.
glossary.sentence: According to Jones (2009), it is a good algorithm for a state space graph with a low depth and branching factor, but can consume large amounts of storage for more complex problems.

glossary.definition: An efficiency technique that can exclude nodes from evaluation and cut off several branches in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: A more interesting issue is the efficiency of alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: A number assigned to nodes at MAX levels in alpha-beta pruning, which should not decrease and defines the best move to maximise the result of the game.
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels.

glossary.definition: A number assigned to nodes at MIN levels in alpha-beta pruning, which should not increase and defines the best move to minimise the result of the game.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels.

glossary.definition: Actions performed by the algorithm based on α and β values to prune unconsidered paths of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two rules to perform cut-offs on the basis of the α and β values (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A process performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the β value of the current node is smaller than the α value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A process performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A specific heuristic measure assigned to a node, for example, 1 to a leaf node where the maximiser wins, or based on the level of the current node and known heuristic measures of direct descendants when all descendants are evaluated.
glossary.sentence: It assigns a precise heuristic measure of 1 to the leaf node, since this is a state in which the maximiser wins the game with a higher score.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned when it is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of an ancestor node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: An approach used to process a partial state space graph that explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: A technique that explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner, going one level deeper at each iteration, used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009).

glossary.definition: The study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: A set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task, explicitly programmed by developers who code each step in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a conventional algorithm is a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that are not based on a precise set of instructions, where the algorithm performs mathematical operations to given data according to a specified model to find the best values of parameters to learn a prediction, solve a classification task or find clusters of data.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning algorithms are not based on a precise set of instructions.

glossary.definition: An item in the dataset that the K-Means algorithm maps to only one cluster.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm maps each data object to only one cluster.

glossary.definition: A collection of data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: A measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A feature (also called an attribute) - a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, for example, the patient’s temperature.

glossary.definition: A specific value of a feature.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: A set of all features characterising a data object.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: An external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training.
glossary.sentence: A hyperparameter (also called a model hyperparameter) - an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training (for example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.).

glossary.definition: A mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: A result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: An internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: A subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: The process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data, etc.
glossary.sentence: Data pre-processing (Step 4) typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data, etc.

glossary.definition: the model that is further used in the model testing based on the test set.
glossary.sentence: The model training based on the given training set leads to the acquisition of the trained model that is further used in the model testing based on the test set.

glossary.definition: checks the model performance.
glossary.sentence: The testing process checks the model performance.

glossary.definition: The mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer.
glossary.sentence: The first topic of this module explained that, in supervised learning, the mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer.

glossary.definition: models on which supervised learning is based because it predicts the output for a new data object based on the model trained on the training set.
glossary.sentence: Thus, supervised learning is based on predictive models because it predicts the output for a new data object based on the model trained on the training set.

glossary.definition: deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal; non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: Searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between two variables: independent one and dependent one.
glossary.sentence: In general, the concept of regression means searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between two variables: independent one and dependent one (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning that uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.
glossary.sentence: In the first topic of Module 4, you learnt that unsupervised learning uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.

glossary.definition: models that unsupervised learning provides to reveal patterns/structure of data.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning provides descriptive models.

glossary.definition: A process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.
glossary.sentence: One of the tasks of unsupervised machine learning is clustering . It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: Subsets of data created by the clustering process, made of one or more data objects and characterised by the similarity of data objects within the cluster and the difference of data objects between the clusters.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.). Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters.

glossary.definition: finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features.
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: reward-based learning, suitable for agents who operate in a changing environment and need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning.

glossary.definition: entities like robots, self-driving cars, or game-playing software who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game- playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: feedback received by an agent if it acts correctly, represented as a numerical value but given with some delay.
glossary.sentence: The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: feedback received by an agent if it acts incorrectly.
glossary.sentence: The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: The data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : a decision process: the data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object.

glossary.definition: Measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.

glossary.definition: Reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : an updating or optimisation process: reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: Generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs. Since the classification is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual class labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted class labels.
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs.

glossary.definition: Demands making predictions in a continuous range, thus its output is a real value. Since the regression is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted labels.
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range.

glossary.definition: The relationship expected between two mentioned variables has a linear pattern and thus can be represented by a straight line in the two-dimensional space.
glossary.sentence: “Linear” means that the relationship expected between the two mentioned variables has a linear pattern and thus can be represented by a straight line in the two-dimensional space, as shown in Figure 1.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression, assuming a linear relationship between input variables (values of data object features) and a single output variable (a prediction), which uses a linear function and learns the values of weights from a given training dataset.
glossary.sentence: Summary of the main characteristics of the linear regression: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression.

glossary.definition: Feature values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Input variables are feature values of data objects.

glossary.definition: A prediction made by the linear regression model, which is a real number in a range from minus infinity to infinity.
glossary.sentence: The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: A real number that corresponds to the intercept, which is a point where the line crosses the y axis or, in other words, it is the value of y when x is equal to 0.
glossary.sentence: It is also usually called a bias term/coefficient .

glossary.definition: The difference between the output value predicted by the linear regression model and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: The difference between the output value predicted by the linear regression model and the real output value is called an error .

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, there are two types of linear regression (Russell and Norvig, 2010): univariate (or simple) linear regression – when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used.

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model is used.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, there are two types of linear regression (Russell and Norvig, 2010): multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: Finding the values of the weights that minimise the empirical loss (error) across all data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: As the task of the linear regression is to find the line or plane (depending on the type of the linear regression) that best fits the data given, then training a linear regression model means finding the values of the weights that minimise the empirical loss (error) across all data objects available in the dataset (Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A loss function used by the linear regression model.
glossary.sentence: The linear regression model uses a loss function called squared loss (also known as L2 loss) (Google Developers, n.d.; Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: The mean square error used to evaluate the total error of the model learnt.
glossary.sentence: In linear regression, the mean square error is used as the cost function to evaluate the total error of the model learnt.

glossary.definition: A metric used to evaluate an algorithm's performance that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: A model for which there is a unique solution because its weights can be calculated using specific equations.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: An optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values, based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.

glossary.definition: a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1
glossary.sentence: It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1.

glossary.definition: When there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive ).

glossary.definition: When there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. It is an extension of binary classification.
