glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning used for regression and classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: It is known that supervised machine learning is used for a regression and a classification task.

glossary.definition: An algorithm used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task, which predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task.

glossary.definition: A mathematical function applied after receiving the linear function's value to squash it within the range of [0,1].
glossary.sentence: Still, after receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.

glossary.definition: A very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: A measure used to determine the similarity of data objects, such as the distance between data objects.
glossary.sentence: To determine the similarity of data objects, a similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: A distance metric used for continuous feature values, where the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated.
glossary.sentence: for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A distance metric used for continuous feature values, where the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated.
glossary.sentence: for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A function that should be minimised.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression has the following cost function , which should also be minimised.

glossary.definition: A distance metric used for categorical feature values that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: An algorithm version (one nearest neighbour) that classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closest data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: An algorithm version where a hyperparameter k is used, which defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: In the kNN version of the algorithm, it defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: A process in the kNN algorithm where, after finding distances and sorting data objects, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and takes the plurality vote of the neighbours.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: One of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning, based on the concepts of distance and a centroid, which works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” . The algorithm is based on two central concepts: the concept of distance; the concept of a centroid.

glossary.definition: The average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster.
glossary.sentence: It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: An algorithm whose task is to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.
glossary.sentence: The task of K-Means is to minimise the sum of distances between data objects belonging to a cluster and the cluster centroid.

glossary.definition: The number of clusters that need to be generated by the K-Means algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm.

glossary.definition: K, which is the number of clusters that need to be generated by the K-Means algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: An iteration when data objects do not change their cluster membership (centroid values do not change).
glossary.sentence: The typical termination criteria of the clustering process in the K-Means algorithm is an iteration when data objects do not change their cluster membership (centroid values do not change) (Jones, 2009; Kubat, 2017; Tyugu , 2007).

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning algorithm that does not demand from the developer any assumption on the number of clusters as the K-Means algorithm does.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is another unsupervised machine learning algorithm that does not demand from the developer any assumption on the number of clusters as the K-Means algorithm does.

glossary.definition: A structure where clusters at each level are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level, with each cluster containing a single data object at the lowest level, and only one cluster containing all of the data objects at the highest level.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.

glossary.definition: A bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: The measure used to choose pairs of groups for merging in agglomerative hierarchical clustering, where the two groups with the smallest dissimilarity are selected.
glossary.sentence: The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: The measure used to choose a split in divisive hierarchical clustering, where the split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest dissimilarity.
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: Methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: A method for deciding about cluster similarity where the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A method for deciding about cluster similarity where the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A method where the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: An output of hierarchical clustering, which is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: Components of a dendrogram that represent merging of data objects into clusters, each having its height.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: Components of a dendrogram that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: The height at which two data objects are merged, serving as an indicator of cluster similarity.
glossary.sentence: The key to interpreting a dendrogram is to focus on the height of clades, that is, the height at which two data objects are merged.

glossary.definition: A line usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.term: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance
glossary.definition: Used to compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: Measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: Applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: The average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: Also known as the coefficient of determination, it indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination.

glossary.definition: Also known as the R2 coefficient, it indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination.

glossary.definition: A data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).
glossary.sentence: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: A data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: A data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).

glossary.definition: A data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category. It represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions. It is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.

glossary.definition: Also called sensitivity and true positive rate, it evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. It is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: An alternative name for recall metric, which evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: An alternative name for recall metric, which evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: calculated for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives.

glossary.definition: calculated for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: After that, we calculate the macro average recall for the task by summing up recall values for all classes and dividing the sum by the number of classes in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a patient suffering from X illness but not diagnosed as such.
glossary.sentence: Kubat (2017) gives an example of a medical diagnosis, in which a patient suffering from X illness but not diagnosed as such represents a false negative, something the doctor wants to avoid—which means that recall should be small.

glossary.definition: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff.

glossary.definition: a metric used in classification tasks that represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff.
glossary.sentence: The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: allows comparing different classifiers and provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve, measuring the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: demands dividing data objects into representative groups, with its output being groups of data objects.
glossary.sentence: In general, the clustering task demands dividing data objects into representative groups. Thus, its output is groups of data objects.

glossary.definition: a metric that does not demand ground-truth labels and represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score. Therefore, it represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters.

glossary.definition: the average distance between each point within a cluster.
glossary.sentence: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: the average distance between all clusters.
glossary.sentence: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: where Silhouette Scores are usually plotted, representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot, representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: cells that the human brain consists of.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons.

glossary.definition: part of a neuron containing a nucleus.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: root-like extensions from the cell body of a neuron that look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: a single root-like extension from the cell body of a neuron that eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.
glossary.sentence: The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.

glossary.definition: the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron, where a sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: signals reaching the neuron's dendrites that promote firing.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: signals reaching the neuron's dendrites that inhibit firing.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: a level of total input to the neuron that, if exceeded, causes the neuron to fire.
glossary.sentence: If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)).

glossary.definition: associated with synapses.
glossary.sentence: Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses.

glossary.definition: a synapse that has a higher conductivity, meaning it must carry more electrical current.
glossary.sentence: It is known that a synapse is made stronger by the however much more electrical current it must carry. Stronger here means that the synapse has a higher conductivity.

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of the neuron defined in 1943 by McCulloch and Pitts, which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks, generally having one or more inputs, a set of real-valued weights, an output, a function for combining input signals, a transfer (activation) function, and a bias input.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons, sometimes also called input signals, which are represented by numerical values, typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset, and are usually labelled by indexed x.
glossary.sentence: one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons.

glossary.definition: A set of real-valued values, one for each input, that express the strength or importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output, serving as the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks, determined in the process of neural network learning, and typically represented by indexed w.
glossary.sentence: a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output.

glossary.definition: A numerical value that can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons, and is usually represented by indexed y.
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: A function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value, typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs, though other functions are also allowed.
glossary.sentence: a function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value.

glossary.definition: A function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.

glossary.definition: A commonly applied input that provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin, which if absent would restrict the line (or the hyperplane) to pass through the origin, and whose weight should be tuned by the learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin.

glossary.definition: The step and sign activation functions, which are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern, capable of performing complicated tasks when many processing units are connected, and exhibiting a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Single neurons within an artificial neural network, also called nodes, that are connected by transmitting signals to each other.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: Single neurons within an artificial neural network, also called processing units, that are connected by transmitting signals to each other.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes.

glossary.definition: Layers of neurons connected to the external environment that only receive input signals from the environment and pass them to the hidden layers, and do not contain computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: The input layer does not contain computing neurons because it only receives inputs signals from the environment and passes them to the hidden layers.

glossary.definition: Layers of neurons connected to the external environment that contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The neurons connected to the external environment form input and output layers.

glossary.definition: The layers of neurons between input and output layers that contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers .

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that has connections only in one direction, where every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units, with no loops, and represents a function of its current input.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir 	• ection .

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs, meaning its activation levels form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour, and its response to a given input depends on its initial state.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs.

glossary.definition: The process related to modifying weights in some particular way, where after processing the input signals in the whole network, if there is a difference between the desired (actual label) output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network, leading to updated weight values for the next processing step.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way .

glossary.definition: The resistance of artificial neural networks to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units, where network operation changes very little, explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network.
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: Another term for fault tolerance, referring to the resistance of artificial neural networks to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units, where network operation changes very little, due to the knowledge of the network being distributed all over the network.
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: identifying the structure or pattern in data
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal; non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.term: input layer (in a single layer perceptron)
glossary.definition: does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing
glossary.sentence: In a single layer perceptron (Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Russell & Norvig, 2010): there is an input layer that does not contain computing neurons and thus does not make processing; there is an output layer containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network.

glossary.term: output layer (in a single layer perceptron)
glossary.definition: containing the computing neurons and providing the final output of the network

glossary.definition: m separate networks because each weight affects only one of the outputs; thus, there will be m separate training processes
glossary.sentence: According to (Russell & Norvig, 2010), the perceptron network with m outputs is really m separate networks because each weight affects only one of the outputs; thus, there will be m separate training processes.

glossary.definition: based on the small adjustments of weights after each training example to reduce the difference between the actual and desired output
glossary.sentence: The learning is based on the small adjustments of weights after each training example to reduce the difference between the actual and desired output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: randomly selected
glossary.sentence: It means that the learning has the following main steps ( Negnevitsky , 2011): the initial weights are randomly selected, and the threshold value usually is set in the range [-0.5; 0.5 ]; a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron; the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit; after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if the predicted output is different from the real output, then the perceptron adjusts the weights; otherwise, if the predicted output is the same as the actual output, then the adjustments of the weights are not made because the performance of the perceptron network is considered to be satisfactory; the perceptron continues the learning process (Steps 2-5) for other examples in the training set until some termination criterion is reached.

glossary.definition: usually computed by applying the delta rule
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule : Therefore, the complete rule for adjustments of the weights of the perceptron is the following one: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.definition: corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers, and does not include computing neurons
glossary.sentence: an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers.

glossary.definition: sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;

glossary.definition: consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be
glossary.sentence: Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.

glossary.definition: pre-defined by the number of input features
glossary.sentence: The input layer is pre-defined by the number of input features.

glossary.definition: pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes
glossary.sentence: The output layer is also pre-defined because the number of its processing units corresponds to the number of classification categories/classes.

glossary.definition: calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only
glossary.sentence: The binary classification task allows a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.

glossary.definition: representing a class of the input object
glossary.sentence: The resulting value received from the processing unit of the output layer is the predicted output value representing a class of the input object.

glossary.definition: propagation of inputs forward in the network
glossary.sentence: performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network;

glossary.definition: backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.definition: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: a difference between the right output and the predicted output
glossary.sentence: Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations.

glossary.definition: A component whose task is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts
glossary.sentence: An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data
glossary.sentence: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: projecting probable consequences of given situations
glossary.sentence: Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains)
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept; procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept; procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Knowledge representation schemas that use a graph where each node represents a complex structure describing an object/concept with its properties and parts, and relations are established using a referencing principle rather than explicit arcs.
glossary.sentence: However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept; procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”)
glossary.sentence: Several kinds of relations are used in semantic networks: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”).

glossary.definition: includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”.

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “Part of”
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8).

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: is represented by the title of “value”
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”.

glossary.definition: allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a feature of semantic networks (Luger, 2009). Inheritance is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node.

glossary.definition: knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts
glossary.sentence: At the same time, semantic networks have disadvantages: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts ); they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways; they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

glossary.definition: A data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation, or a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept.
glossary.sentence: A frame is “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation” (Minsky, 1975).

glossary.definition: Properties of objects/concepts within a frame.
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames that represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A source of slot values where many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: initialization – many slot values are assigned during the creation of the knowledge base;

glossary.definition: A source of slot values where intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values.
glossary.sentence: external programs – nowadays intelligent systems communicate with external programs like databases or expert systems that can deliver slot values;

glossary.definition: A source of slot values where procedures and methods are assigned to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed.
glossary.sentence: procedural attachments to frames – it is possible to assign procedures and methods to slots that, for example, will calculate a slot value when it is needed (see further in the text );

glossary.definition: A source of slot values where the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation.
glossary.sentence: user – the intelligent system can demand the user to provide slot values during the system’s operation;

glossary.definition: A source of slot values where frames support inheritance of slots and their values.
glossary.sentence: inheritance – frames support inheritance of slots and their values (see further in the text).

glossary.definition: An expert system where frames are used to capture and represent knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Frames are used to capture and represent knowledge in a frame-based expert system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Collections of related frames linked together.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), “collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems” .

glossary.definition: A type of frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: Classes that are derived from other classes.
glossary.sentence: Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5).

glossary.definition: A frame that is an instance for a class frame or sub-class frame.
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: A method to relate two frames by using the unique title of one frame as a slot value in another frame.
glossary.sentence: This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A relation defined between instances and their classes or sub-classes, represented as a Class slot in sub-class frames and instance frames.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal . It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: A type of relation that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts, where frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .

glossary.definition: A type of relation that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: The possibility for sub-class and instance frames to inherit slots and their values from class frames.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: When a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously, allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Procedures that are invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base, triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed, and control actions for slot-level manipulations.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Frames that are acquired when demons are assigned to them.
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A common demon that is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it.
glossary.sentence: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: A common demon that is triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: A common demon that is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: A common demon that is triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: A structure that stores knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way, provides hierarchical organization, allows representation of various knowledge, supports inheritance, and allows adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and ensure the knowledge base's integrity.
glossary.sentence: Frames have the following advantages: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: A component within a frame that can contain a calculated value.
glossary.sentence: The frame Card#2967 contains the calculated value for the slot Ensured services “Luggage insurance for free ” ; The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: An executable demon that calculates the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount is requested.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: A component that acquires knowledge about some object/concept by finding a corresponding frame and checking its slots.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots.

glossary.definition: A high-level frame, such as Person and Animal, from which sub-class frames can inherit slots.
glossary.sentence: First, there are two high-level class frames: Person and Animal.

glossary.definition: A frame that inherits slots from a class frame and can have new slots added.
glossary.sentence: Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog. They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added: Affectionate in the Cat sub-class and Roar and Barking in the Dog sub-class.

glossary.definition: Relations between frames that describe associations, such as Owns, Animal owner, or Afraid of.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex , the relation Owns , and Rex- TreeAlex , the relation Animal owner; Tom – Rex , the relation Afraid of .

glossary.definition: Components that can be added to slots to control access to slot values and ensure the knowledge base's integrity.
glossary.sentence: Frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Schemas used to develop rule-based systems that represent a set of problem-solving instructions, not knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems. They represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: The simplest type of rule, which is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem.
glossary.sentence: The simplest type of rules is a production rule. Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The "IF" part of a production rule.
glossary.sentence: A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: The "THEN" part of a production rule, which can be an action, statement, or conclusion.
glossary.sentence: A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: An intelligent system acquired if statements are added to the THEN part of rules.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: An intelligent system acquired if the THEN part of rules contains actions.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems that consist of a knowledge base containing a set of production rules, a working memory, and an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A component that typically includes many production rules interrelated by sharing knowledge, where knowledge units in the THEN part of one rule make the IF part of another rule.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base typically includes many production rules interrelated by sharing knowledge: knowledge units in the THEN part of one rule make the IF part of another rule (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: A component that keeps track of the current state of the problem-solving process (case-specific data) by storing facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived, confidence measures, information from external programs, and dead ends in the search process.
glossary.sentence: The rule-based expert system must keep track of the current state of the problem-solving process (case-specific data) and the working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A component that performs the control cycle "Recognize-act" to link the rules in the knowledge base with facts in the working memory, thus carrying out reasoning by interpreting and applying rules to reach a solution.
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle "Recognize-act" (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: A component of an expert system that should explain its reasoning and justify the output.
glossary.sentence: Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A component through which the user interacts with the system, designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface.
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Additional components that may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An interface intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules, modify, and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process contained in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: A cycle that matches a pattern against either the IF part (in data-driven search) or the THEN part (in goal-driven search) of production rules in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search... the control cycle "Recognize-act" matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Production rules that are part of the conflict set, which are acquired when several rules match the pattern.
glossary.sentence: if several rules match the pattern, a conflict set is acquired. Production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled.

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A strategy used for the purpose of conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction ); the control cycle repeats with the modified working memory; searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory.

glossary.definition: One of the enabled rules that is executed, changing the content of the working memory by adding its IF or THEN part.
glossary.sentence: For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction ); the control cycle repeats with the modified working memory; searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory.

glossary.definition: A natural way of knowledge representation, often explained by human experts using “if... then...” constructions.
glossary.sentence: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” ( Negnevitsky , 2011 ).

glossary.definition: Methods used to make the search more effective, including control through the selection of search direction, rule sequence and structure, and conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: A component where problem descriptions, given data, or goals (patterns) are added during the search process.
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search... the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: A method of conflict resolution based on the assumption that a more specific rule processes more information than a general one, often meaning a rule with more conditions.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An advantage meaning rules have no syntactic interactions, affecting other rules only by changing patterns in working memory, without direct calls or variable setting.
glossary.sentence: Modularity of production rules. Rules have no syntactic interactions.

glossary.definition: An advantage where knowledge is stored in the knowledge base and processed by the inference engine, allowing independent modification of either without affecting the other.
glossary.sentence: Separation of knowledge and control. Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: The ease of following the execution of production rules due to their modularity and iterative nature, allowing display of selected rules and meaningful explanations of system state and action.
glossary.sentence: Tracing and explanation. The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules.

glossary.definition: Knowledge that most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with by adding confidence factors.
glossary.sentence: Dealing with incomplete and uncertain knowledge. Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.
